text: Black Rot is a fungal disease caused by Guignardia bidwellii that attacks grapevines in warm, humid climates. It is one of the most economically damaging diseases in viticulture, capable of destroying an entire harvest within a single season if left unmanaged. The disease affects leaves, shoots, and berries, but its most devastating and characteristic effect is on the fruit, which turns completely black and shrivels into hard, mummified balls that remain attached to the cluster. It is widespread in humid grape-growing regions worldwide and is considered a primary threat to European wine grape varieties.

Black Rot is rated as a very high danger disease. It does not kill the grapevine itself, but it can destroy one hundred percent of the fruit crop in a single season under warm, wet conditions. Yield losses of this magnitude have been documented in unmanaged vineyards. The speed of damage is fast once infection begins, with berries moving from healthy to fully mummified within one to two weeks. The economic impact is among the highest of any grape disease worldwide, affecting both fresh market and wine grape production.

The incubation period ranges from eight to twenty-five days depending on temperature, with the fastest development at around twenty-six degrees Celsius. The most critical infection window is from bloom through four weeks after bloom, during which young berries are highly susceptible. Once a berry is infected, visible symptoms appear within one to three weeks, and mummification follows within another one to two weeks. The disease spreads fastest during warm, wet spring and early summer weather coinciding with the post-bloom period.

Black Rot overwinters in mummified berries and infected canes left from the previous season. In spring, rainfall triggers the release of ascospores from overwintering structures in the old mummies and infected wood. These spores are carried by wind and rain splash to young leaves, shoots, and developing berries. Young berries are the most vulnerable tissue and can be infected from bloom until about four weeks after bloom. After primary infections establish, the fungus produces secondary spores called conidia from new lesions, which continue spreading through the season via wind and rain. Mummified berries left on the vine or on the ground are the single most important source of inoculum for the following season.

Guignardia bidwellii is an ascomycete fungus with two reproductive stages. The sexual stage produces ascospores inside overwintering structures called pseudothecia, which form in mummified berries and infected canes. These ascospores are the primary inoculum released in spring during rainfall. The asexual stage produces conidia from pycnidia that form in leaf lesions during the growing season, providing secondary inoculum throughout the summer. The fungus penetrates host tissue through the cuticle and stomata and requires free water on the tissue surface for infection. It does not persist in soil.

On leaves, Black Rot produces small yellowish spots that enlarge into reddish-brown circular lesions between two and ten millimeters in diameter. Each lesion has a dark brown border and contains small black pycnidia arranged in concentric rings inside the lesion. Severely infected leaves may drop prematurely.

On shoots and petioles, the fungus causes dark brown to black elongated lesions that become sunken and shriveled. These can girdle and kill young shoots when infection is severe.

On berries, small white spots appear first, then the berry turns entirely brown and then black. The infected berry shrivels into a hard, black mummified form that remains attached to the cluster. This mummification is the hallmark of Black Rot and is unmistakable once recognized.

On cluster stems and tendrils, dark shrunken lesions can cut off water supply to entire bunches, accelerating berry collapse.

Black Rot thrives in temperatures between ten and thirty-two degrees Celsius, with the optimum around twenty-six degrees. The fungus requires a minimum of four hours of free water on the tissue surface to complete infection. At twenty-six degrees this threshold is met after just six hours of wetness, while at ten degrees it requires twenty-four hours. The disease is most severe during warm, humid summers with frequent rainfall, especially when these conditions coincide with the bloom and post-bloom period.

The cycle begins with the fungus overwintering inside mummified berries and infected canes. Spring rains trigger the release of ascospores, which infect young tissue during wet periods. After an incubation of eight to twenty-five days, lesions form on leaves and shoots. Pycnidia within these lesions release conidia that spread through wind and rain for secondary infections throughout the season. Berries become infected primarily during the critical post-bloom window. Infected berries mummify and either remain on the vine or fall to the ground, carrying the fungus into the next season.

Cultural management begins with the removal and destruction of all mummified berries during dormant pruning. This single action has the greatest impact on reducing next season's inoculum. Infected canes should also be removed. Canopy airflow should be improved through shoot positioning, leaf removal, and proper training systems to reduce humidity and accelerate drying after rain. Overhead irrigation should be avoided, and fallen leaf material should be cleared from the vineyard floor after harvest.

Variety selection plays a significant role. American grape varieties such as Concord and Niagara have moderate natural resistance, while most European Vitis vinifera varieties including Chardonnay, Merlot, and Cabernet Sauvignon are highly susceptible. Disease-resistant hybrid varieties such as Marquette and Frontenac offer good to excellent resistance and are valuable where variety flexibility exists.

Chemical control should begin at budbreak with a protectant fungicide and continue on a seven-day interval from bloom through four weeks post-bloom. This is the most critical spray window and should not be skipped. After the critical window, intervals can extend to ten to fourteen days in dry weather. Fungicide classes used include multi-site protectants such as mancozeb and captan, systemic triazoles such as myclobutanil and tebuconazole, strobilurins such as azoxystrobin, and SDHI products such as boscalid. Modes of action must be rotated every two to three sprays to prevent fungicide resistance. Strobilurins should never be used alone and should always be mixed with a protectant. In organic programs, copper-based fungicides and captan are the primary options and must be applied on tight schedules given their purely protective activity.

If no symptoms are present before bloom, apply a protective fungicide and remove any old mummies immediately. During the bloom period in wet weather, spray every seven days without exception. When first leaf spots appear, spray immediately and inspect all berries closely. When first infected berries are spotted, remove the infected clusters and spray remaining clusters with a systemic fungicide. After harvest, remove all mummies from vines and ground and destroy them.

Scout weekly from budbreak through six weeks post-bloom. Focus inspection on berries, leaf undersides, and shoot tips. Assess how thoroughly mummies were removed before the season, as incompletely cleaned vineyards carry dramatically higher risk. After rain events during the critical window, inspect within three to five days for the first leaf lesions. Use disease forecasting models to optimize spray timing where available.

Black Rot is most severe in humid continental climates, particularly in the eastern United States and central Europe. It is present wherever grapevines are grown commercially but causes less damage in dry Mediterranean and Pacific Coast climates where warm, wet spring conditions are less common.

My berries turned black and shriveled. Is this definitely Black Rot? Hard, mummified black berries are the hallmark of Black Rot. Botrytis also causes berry rot but produces soft, gray fuzzy berries, not hard black mummies. If berries are hard and black, it is almost certainly Black Rot.

I missed the critical spray window. Is it too late? If berries are already mummifying, you cannot reverse the damage. Focus on removing infected clusters to reduce further sporulation and prepare aggressively for next season.

Do I need to spray if the weather has been dry? Black Rot needs at least four hours of leaf wetness to infect. In dry weather the risk drops significantly. Monitor weather and spray before rain events during the critical window.

How do I stop it from coming back next year? Remove all mummified berries and infected canes during dormant pruning. This is the single most important action. Combine it with early-season fungicide timing for comprehensive protection.

Can Black Rot infect through wounds? Yes. Wounds from hail, bird damage, or mechanical harvesting create additional entry points. After hail events, apply a fungicide within twenty-four hours.

Common Rust is a fungal disease of corn caused by Puccinia sorghi, a rust fungus that produces distinctive brick-red powdery pustules scattered across both surfaces of leaves. It is one of the most widespread corn diseases globally, present wherever corn is grown. While it appears alarming due to the vivid coloration of the pustules, it is rarely devastating on field corn when resistant hybrids are used. On sweet corn and highly susceptible hybrids, however, it can cause significant defoliation and yield loss. The pathogen is an obligate parasite that requires living host tissue to survive and cannot persist in soil or dead debris.

The danger level of Common Rust is moderate on field corn and rises to high on sweet corn and susceptible hybrids. The disease rarely kills plants directly but reduces photosynthesis and impairs grain fill. Yield losses on susceptible field corn hybrids typically range from five to twenty percent, while losses on sweet corn can reach forty percent. Resistant field corn varieties experience minimal loss. The economic impact is moderate on field corn and high on sweet corn and popcorn. Once infected, tissue cannot be cured and retains pustules for the rest of the season.

Under optimal cool and humid conditions, Common Rust takes seven to fourteen days from infection to visible symptoms. The fungus produces new spore generations every seven to ten days, allowing the disease to cycle rapidly through a field. From the first pustules to severe infection takes two to four weeks in cool, humid weather. Spread stops quickly when temperatures become hot and dry, which is an important characteristic that distinguishes it from more aggressive diseases.

Common Rust spreads almost exclusively through wind-dispersed urediospores that travel from infected fields, often originating hundreds of kilometers away in tropical or subtropical regions where the fungus survives year-round. In temperate climates, the fungus cannot overwinter on corn residue and must be reintroduced each season from warmer areas. Once spores land on corn leaves, they germinate in the presence of moisture, penetrate through stomata without requiring any wound, and establish infection. Within a field, each pustule produces millions of new spores that spread to neighboring plants through wind. Rotation and tillage do not reduce risk because inoculum arrives from distant sources, not from local residue.

Puccinia sorghi is a basidiomycete rust fungus with a complex life cycle involving two hosts. On corn, it produces urediospores that drive epidemic spread and teliospores that form late in the season. Teliospores infect the alternate host, Oxalis species or wood sorrel, on which sexual recombination produces aeciospores that can re-infect corn. In tropical and subtropical regions this full cycle maintains year-round populations. In temperate regions the alternate host cycle is less epidemiologically important. The fungus is an obligate biotroph, meaning it grows only in living tissue and cannot be cultured on artificial media. This makes it difficult to study in the laboratory but also means it dies with its host in cold climates.

On leaves, Common Rust produces small, circular to elongate pustules that are brick-red to cinnamon-brown in color and appear on both the upper and lower leaf surfaces. This bilateral distribution is an important diagnostic feature that distinguishes Common Rust from Southern Rust, which produces pustules mainly on the upper surface. The pustules rupture the leaf epidermis and release a powdery mass of brick-red urediospores. Later in the season, pustules darken to brown or black as teliospores replace urediospores. In severe infections, leaves turn yellow and then brown and die prematurely. Sheaths and husks may also be infected in very susceptible hybrids under heavy pressure.

Common Rust thrives in cool temperatures between sixteen and twenty-three degrees Celsius, with the optimum around twenty degrees. Dew, fog, or rain on leaf surfaces is required for spore germination. The disease is most severe from the V10 growth stage through silking, and late-planted corn is at highest risk because it passes through the susceptible stage when inoculum levels are at their peak. High-elevation tropical corn production is particularly vulnerable. Cool nights with heavy dew are a strong predictor of active infection.

Each season in temperate regions, urediospores arrive via wind from southern or tropical areas. They land on corn leaves, germinate in moisture, and penetrate through stomata. Pustules develop within seven to fourteen days and release new urediospores. Multiple infection cycles repeat throughout the season, each cycle seven to ten days long. Late in the season, dark teliospores form within pustules. In regions where both A1 and A2 mating types coexist, sexual recombination on the Oxalis alternate host generates new races capable of overcoming host resistance genes.

The most effective management strategy is planting hybrids with strong rust resistance ratings. Hybrids rated six or above on a nine-point scale tolerate substantial rust pressure without significant yield loss. Quantitative or partial resistance, which is controlled by multiple genes, is more durable than single-gene resistance because it is harder for new fungal races to overcome completely.

Cultural practices have limited value against Common Rust because inoculum arrives from wind rather than from local fields. Planting early allows the crop to pass through the most susceptible vegetative stages before peak inoculum arrives. Removing volunteer corn plants reduces early-season infection sources. Balanced nutrition, particularly adequate phosphorus and potassium, supports plant tolerance.

Fungicide applications are economically justified on susceptible hybrids and sweet corn when disease reaches the ear leaf or above at or before tasseling. Effective fungicide classes include triazoles such as propiconazole and tebuconazole, strobilurins such as azoxystrobin and pyraclostrobin, and premix products combining SDHI and triazole chemistries. The optimal timing is between the VT and R1 growth stages. A single well-timed application is usually sufficient. On sweet corn the threshold is much lower and fungicide should be applied as soon as rust appears before or at tasseling. Applications after the R3 milk stage have very limited economic return on field corn. Modes of action should be alternated to reduce resistance risk.

Biological control options currently have minimal practical value against corn rust. Bacillus-based biofungicides show little activity, and no commercially effective biological product exists for this disease.

Before planting, choose a hybrid with a rust resistance rating of six or higher out of nine. During early vegetative stages when pustules are present, monitor closely but do not spray because plants can tolerate early rust without economic loss. When approaching tasseling with rust visible on the ear leaf, apply a triazole or premix fungicide immediately. On sweet corn at any stage with rust visible, apply fungicide promptly because sweet corn tolerates much less pressure. After tasseling in a severely infected field, apply fungicide only if the crop is before the R3 dough stage.

Begin scouting at the V8 growth stage and intensify from V10 through R2. Sample at least twenty plants per field at multiple locations throughout the field. Record the percentage of plants showing rust and note the highest leaf position where pustules are present. The key threshold for fungicide application is disease on the ear leaf or above at tasseling. Monitor weather for cool nights below twenty degrees Celsius combined with dew, which signals high infection risk. Regional rust forecasting maps are available in some areas and can guide scouting priorities.

Common Rust is present globally wherever corn is grown. It is most severe in tropical highlands such as East Africa, Central America, and the Andean region of South America. In temperate regions of North America and Europe, severity varies year to year depending on how early inoculum arrives from southern source regions and whether cool, humid weather follows. Late-planted fields and high-elevation areas within any region are at the greatest risk.

Is Common Rust the same as Southern Rust? No. Southern Rust is caused by Puccinia polysora and is more aggressive. Common Rust pustules appear on both leaf surfaces while Southern Rust pustules are mostly on the upper surface. Southern Rust pustules are also more orange in color and smaller in size.

The rust appeared suddenly overnight. Is that normal? Yes. Wind-carried spores can arrive in large numbers overnight, and cool, dewy nights cause rapid infection. What looks like a sudden outbreak is often the visible result of infections that occurred seven to fourteen days earlier.

My field was rotated away from corn. Why do I still have rust? Because Common Rust inoculum comes from wind, not from local soil or residue. Rotation does not protect against this disease.

Can Common Rust cause stalk rot? Indirectly. Severe rust reduces photosynthesis and forces the plant to remobilize stalk carbohydrates to fill grain, weakening stalks and increasing susceptibility to Gibberella and Fusarium stalk rots and subsequent lodging.

Does rust affect grain quality or safety? Rust does not produce mycotoxins. It reduces yield and test weight but does not make grain unsafe for food or feed.

Gray Leaf Spot is one of the most yield-limiting fungal diseases of corn worldwide, caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis. It produces long, rectangular, gray to tan lesions that run parallel to leaf veins, eventually causing entire leaves to blight and die. The disease is particularly destructive in no-till production systems where infected corn residue remains on the soil surface as the primary source of inoculum season after season. It thrives in warm, humid environments with prolonged overnight dew and poor air circulation through the canopy. Severe infections can destroy fifty percent or more of yield by killing the leaf canopy before grain fill is complete, and they also predispose plants to serious stalk rot at harvest.

Gray Leaf Spot is rated as a high-danger disease. It does not kill plants directly, but severe defoliation before grain fill is complete prevents ears from developing normally and causes premature plant death through stalk rot. Yield losses range from twenty to fifty percent in severe cases, and losses exceeding fifty percent have been recorded in highly susceptible hybrids under ideal disease conditions. The disease reduces grain weight, test weight, and stalk integrity at harvest. Once lesions form, fungicides cannot reverse existing damage but can protect healthy remaining tissue.

Infection to visible symptoms takes fourteen to twenty-one days under optimal warm, humid conditions. The fungus spreads continuously through wind during the growing season, and spores produced within lesions generate new infection cycles. From the first lesions to severe canopy blighting takes three to six weeks under favorable conditions. The disease progresses at a moderate to fast pace and is cumulative, meaning that without intervention it tends to worsen steadily rather than plateau.

The primary source of Gray Leaf Spot inoculum is infected corn residue left on the soil surface. This is the defining characteristic that separates it from diseases whose inoculum arrives on wind from distant regions. The fungus survives as dormant structures in residue for one to two seasons and produces conidia during warm, humid conditions that wind carries to lower leaves. No-till and reduced-till fields have dramatically higher risk because residue is retained on the surface rather than buried. Once lesions establish on lower leaves, they produce new conidia that spread upward through the canopy progressively over the season. Long-distance spread by wind does occur across fields and regions during warm, humid weather, but local residue is always the dominant source.

Cercospora zeae-maydis is an ascomycete fungus that survives as stromata in corn residue on the soil surface. A closely related species, Cercospora zeina, has been identified particularly in African corn production. The fungus produces long, multiseptate conidia that are dispersed by wind. Infection requires penetration through stomata and depends on extended leaf wetness of at least twelve hours. The fungus produces a photosensitizing toxin called cercosporin that assists tissue colonization by generating reactive oxygen species in the presence of light. Residue on the soil surface can remain infectious for one to two growing seasons, which is why rotation away from corn reduces disease pressure over time.

On leaves, Gray Leaf Spot begins as small tan lesions with yellow halos that rapidly expand into rectangular, gray to tan streaks one to six centimeters long. The lesion margins are defined by leaf veins, giving the characteristic rectangular shape that is the primary diagnostic feature of this disease. As the fungus sporulates, lesions develop a velvety gray appearance on the surface. Severe infection causes complete leaf blighting with premature plant death. Sheaths may also develop similar rectangular lesions in severe cases. The disease progresses upward through the canopy from lower to upper leaves, and the most critical timing is when it reaches the ear leaf and above before grain fill begins.

Gray Leaf Spot thrives in temperatures between twenty-two and thirty degrees Celsius with an optimum around twenty-five degrees. It requires at least twelve hours of continuous leaf wetness for infection, which is most commonly provided by prolonged overnight dew in dense canopies. High relative humidity throughout the night is a strong predictor of active infection. The disease is most severe during the period from tasseling through grain fill. No-till fields, dense plant populations, and late-planted corn are consistently the highest-risk situations. Fields in low-lying areas or river bottoms with poor air drainage are particularly vulnerable.

The cycle begins with the fungus surviving the winter in corn residue on the soil surface. When warm, humid spring conditions arrive, conidia are produced on the residue and wind carries them to the lower leaves of young corn plants. Infection occurs during extended leaf wetness periods. Lesions develop and begin producing new conidia within fourteen to twenty-one days. The disease progresses upward through the canopy through repeated infection cycles driven by wind-dispersed spores and overnight dew. At the end of the season, the fungus returns to residue on the soil surface where it persists until the following season.

The most effective management tool is planting hybrids with high gray leaf spot resistance ratings. Hybrids rated four to seven on a nine-point scale provide meaningful protection, though no completely immune hybrid exists because resistance is polygenic and partial. In no-till fields with a history of severe disease, selecting resistant hybrids is especially important and should be the first management decision.

Cultural practices that reduce residue inoculum are highly valuable. Rotating to a non-host crop such as soybeans or small grains for one to two seasons allows residue to break down and substantially reduces the inoculum load. Tillage that buries residue reduces primary inoculum but must be weighed against erosion concerns. Avoiding excessively dense plant populations reduces canopy humidity. Adequate potassium fertility supports plant tolerance. Planting early allows the canopy to senesce before the worst humidity periods of late summer.

Fungicide applications are economically justified when disease reaches the ear leaf by tasseling in susceptible hybrids. The optimal timing is at VT to R1, meaning at tasseling through silking. Strobilurins such as azoxystrobin and pyraclostrobin provide good protectant activity. Triazoles such as propiconazole and tebuconazole offer systemic and some curative activity. SDHI products such as fluxapyroxad are excellent and are most effective when used in premix combinations with triazoles or strobilurins. One application at the correct timing is usually sufficient. A second application at R2 may be justified in severe epidemic years on highly susceptible hybrids. Applying after the R4 dough stage has negligible economic return. Fungicide modes of action should be rotated to reduce resistance development.

Biological options including Trichoderma-based products and silicon amendments have limited and inconsistent efficacy and are not primary management tools. Crop rotation and resistant varieties remain the most practical and cost-effective approach, especially in no-till production.

Before planting in a high-risk field such as no-till with previous corn, select a resistant hybrid and consider rotating to a non-host crop. From V6 to V10 when lesions appear on lower leaves, begin scouting every five days. When approaching tasseling with lesions on the ear leaf or above, apply fungicide immediately because this is the critical window. After tasseling with severe canopy blighting, apply fungicide if the crop is still between R2 and R3. After harvest in a severely affected field, consider deep tillage or rotation to reduce residue inoculum for the next season.

Begin scouting at V10 and intensify from V14 through tasseling. Sample a minimum of twenty plants per field at five randomly selected locations. Record the percentage of leaf area affected and note the highest leaf position showing lesions. Low-lying and foggy areas of fields often show disease earliest. Scout these areas first. The key threshold for fungicide application is gray leaf spot visible on the ear leaf approaching or at tasseling in a susceptible hybrid.

Gray Leaf Spot is a major disease in the Americas, particularly in the US Corn Belt and in Brazil and Argentina. It is also a significant problem in sub-Saharan Africa, especially in highland tropical maize production, and in parts of Asia including India and China. The disease is most destructive in warm, humid highland environments and in no-till production systems wherever corn is grown.

Can Gray Leaf Spot kill my corn plants? Not directly. But severe defoliation before grain fill causes ears to develop poorly, and infected plants become highly prone to stalk rot, which causes lodging before or during harvest.

Is one fungicide application enough? In most years yes, one well-timed application at VT to R1 provides sufficient protection through grain fill. A second application at R2 may be warranted in severe epidemic years with highly susceptible hybrids.

The disease only appeared on the bottom two leaves. Should I spray? Lower leaf infection alone rarely justifies a spray. Spray when disease reaches the ear leaf approaching or at tasseling.

Can I distinguish Gray Leaf Spot from Northern Corn Leaf Blight? Yes. Gray Leaf Spot lesions are rectangular, gray, and confined between veins, usually less than six centimeters long. Northern Corn Leaf Blight lesions are large, five to fifteen centimeters, cigar-shaped, tan, and not restricted by veins.

Does nitrogen fertilization affect severity? Moderate, balanced nitrogen is beneficial. Both deficiency and excess can increase susceptibility. Adequate potassium is the most important nutrient for disease tolerance.

Early Blight is a fungal disease of potato and tomato caused by Alternaria solani. It is one of the most common and widely distributed foliar diseases of potato worldwide. The disease creates dark spots with distinctive concentric rings that form a bullseye or target pattern, beginning on the oldest lower leaves and progressing steadily upward through the plant. Beyond leaf damage, Early Blight also infects potato tubers, producing sunken corky spots that reduce marketability and increase storage losses. The disease is significantly worsened by plant stress, especially from low nitrogen or drought, and is considered a mid to late season disease that builds as the canopy closes and plants begin to age.

Early Blight is rated as moderate to high danger depending on crop condition and management. It rarely kills plants directly, but severe defoliation dramatically reduces tuber yield by cutting off photosynthesis before bulking is complete. Yield losses in severe, unmanaged cases range from twenty to forty percent. Tuber quality is also reduced when surface lesions occur, creating entry points for secondary pathogens during storage. The disease is significantly more damaging on stressed crops, particularly those suffering from nitrogen deficiency or drought, making plant nutrition and irrigation management inseparable from disease management.

The incubation period is two to three days in warm, humid conditions and extends to up to seven days in cooler weather. The disease builds from mid-season onward, worsening after canopy closure when humidity within the canopy increases. It progresses from the oldest leaves upward over three to six weeks under favorable conditions. The speed of progression is moderate under normal circumstances but accelerates dramatically when plants are under nitrogen or water stress.

Early Blight overwinters in infected plant debris in the soil and on infected seed tubers. Conidia are dispersed by wind and rain splash from lesions and from debris on the soil surface. The fungus enters host tissue through stomata or by directly penetrating weakened cuticle, especially on older and stressed tissue. Drought stress and low nitrogen dramatically increase susceptibility by weakening plant defenses. Each new lesion produces thousands of fresh conidia within days, providing continuous secondary inoculum throughout the season. Spores can also travel by wind between fields over significant distances.

Alternaria solani is an ascomycete fungus that survives in infected plant debris in soil and on infected seed tubers for one to two seasons. It produces large, club-shaped, multicellular conidia with a characteristic long beak, which are readily carried by wind. The fungus also produces toxins including altersolanol and solanopyrone that contribute to tissue necrosis by killing cells ahead of the advancing mycelium. This toxin production explains why lesions expand rapidly even when the fungus itself has not yet colonized the full lesion area. The pathogen infects both potato and tomato and can move between the two crops, making proximity of these crops a risk factor.

On leaves, Early Blight produces dark brown to black lesions with distinct concentric rings that create the classic bullseye or target pattern. A yellow chlorotic halo surrounds each lesion. Lesions range from three to fifteen millimeters in diameter. The disease begins exclusively on older, lower leaves and progresses upward over the season. Severely infected leaves turn yellow and drop, reducing the photosynthetic capacity of the plant.

On stems, dark elongated sunken lesions can appear, occasionally girdling young stems and causing shoot death. Petiole lesions may cause premature leaf drop before the leaf itself is fully killed.

On tubers, Early Blight produces dark, sunken, circular to irregular corky lesions on the surface that are five to fifteen millimeters in diameter with slightly raised margins. Below the skin, a shallow, dry, corky rot is visible. These lesions reduce marketability significantly and serve as entry points for secondary pathogens during storage.

Early Blight thrives in temperatures between twenty-four and twenty-nine degrees Celsius with an optimum around twenty-seven degrees. High humidity and periods of leaf wetness are required for infection. The disease is most damaging on senescent, stressed, or nitrogen-deficient plants. It builds particularly after canopy closure when humidity within the crop increases. Dense plantings and mid to late season timing are consistent risk factors.

The cycle begins with the fungus overwintering in infected plant debris in the soil and on infected seed tubers. When warm, humid conditions arrive in the growing season, conidia are released from debris and dispersed by wind and rain splash. Infection occurs through stomata or directly through weakened cuticle on older tissue. After an incubation of two to seven days, new lesions form and immediately begin producing fresh conidia. Multiple cycles repeat throughout the season. At the end of the season, infected debris falls to the soil and perpetuates the cycle, while infected tubers can also carry the pathogen into the next season and into storage.

Plant nutrition management is the most critical non-chemical tool for Early Blight. Maintaining adequate nitrogen throughout the season is essential because nitrogen-deficient plants develop far more severe disease under the same inoculum levels as well-fertilized plants. Potassium adequacy also supports disease tolerance. Consistent irrigation to prevent drought stress is equally important, and drip irrigation is preferred over overhead systems to keep foliage dry.

Crop rotation away from potato, tomato, and other solanaceous hosts for two to three seasons reduces debris inoculum in the soil. Certified disease-free seed tubers should always be used. Removing infected plant debris after harvest and early haulm destruction before harvest reduce tuber infection pressure.

Fungicide applications should begin when disease first appears or when conditions consistently favor infection, which means warm temperatures combined with humid nights. Applications every seven to ten days are typical, increasing to every five to seven days during wet, warm periods. Multi-site protectants such as chlorothalonil and mancozeb are applied preventively. Systemic fungicides including strobilurins such as azoxystrobin and pyraclostrobin, triazoles such as difenoconazole and tebuconazole, and SDHI products provide both preventive and curative activity and are valuable when disease is already present. Modes of action must be rotated with every two applications because strobilurin resistance in Alternaria populations is increasingly documented. Thorough coverage of lower canopy leaves is important because this is where the disease initiates.

In organic programs, copper-based fungicides are the most reliable option. Bacillus subtilis products have some suppressive activity and can support an integrated program. Stress management through nutrition and irrigation remains the most impactful organic intervention.

When bullseye spots appear on lower leaves, apply fungicide and check nitrogen levels immediately. When plants show drought stress alongside disease, irrigate first and then apply fungicide after irrigation. Before hot weather above twenty-four degrees combined with humid nights, apply a protectant fungicide in advance. When disease is spreading rapidly up the canopy, switch to a systemic fungicide and address all stress factors simultaneously. At harvest, when tubers show corky spots, assess and improve the rotation plan and storage conditions for the following season.

Begin scouting at canopy closure and intensify mid-season. Sample twenty to thirty plants per field from multiple locations. Record the percentage of plants with symptoms, the highest leaf position affected, and the number of lesions per leaf. Check the oldest leaves at the base of the plant first because disease always begins there. If leaf color is pale or yellow alongside Early Blight symptoms, nitrogen correction is urgent. Track weather periods of three or more consecutive days with temperatures above twenty-four degrees combined with rain or heavy dew, as these represent high-risk windows.

Early Blight is present globally wherever potato and tomato are grown. It is most severe in warm, humid continental and subtropical climates. In temperate regions it is a mid to late season disease. In tropical production regions it operates as a year-round problem. It is consistently among the most common foliar diseases reported by potato growers worldwide.

How is Potato Early Blight different from Late Blight? Early Blight produces bullseye spots with concentric rings, starts on old lower leaves, and spreads over weeks with no white mold on the leaf underside. Late Blight produces irregular water-soaked spots with white mold on the leaf underside, spreads in days, and can collapse an entire field in one to two weeks. Late Blight is an emergency requiring immediate action.

My nitrogen is good but I still have Early Blight. Why? Nitrogen is one factor. Other stresses including drought, compacted soil, heavy crop load, and potassium deficiency also increase susceptibility. Evaluate overall plant health comprehensively.

Do tubers from infected plants store well? Tubers with Early Blight lesions are more vulnerable to secondary rots in storage. Harvest at proper maturity, cure at twelve to fifteen degrees Celsius for two weeks, and store in cool, dry, well-ventilated conditions.

Can Early Blight spread from potatoes to my tomatoes? Yes. Alternaria solani infects both crops. Avoid planting potatoes and tomatoes adjacent to each other and manage both under the same disease program.

The fungicide I used stopped working. Why? Strobilurin resistance in Alternaria is increasingly common. Switch to or rotate with a multi-site protectant such as mancozeb or chlorothalonil, or use a triazole fungicide from a different mode of action group.

Esca is a complex and incurable wood disease of grapevines caused by a consortium of fungi that slowly colonize and rot the internal woody tissue of the trunk and arms. It has been recognized in vineyards for over two thousand years and remains one of the most serious threats to mature vines worldwide. On the outside, the disease produces a striking tiger-stripe pattern on leaves and dark spots on berries known as black measles. Internally, the wood decays progressively until the vine's vascular system can no longer function. In its most severe acute form, called apoplexy, a vine can collapse and die within two to five days during a summer heat wave. Because the wood cannot be disinfected once infected, Esca is defined by prevention rather than cure.

Esca is rated as very high danger and is unique in being both chronic and incurable. It can kill grapevines through two distinct pathways. The chronic form causes gradual decline over years as internal wood decay reduces the vine's ability to transport water and nutrients. The acute form, apoplexy, causes sudden total collapse of the vine within days when heat stress pushes a severely infected vine past its physiological limit. Yield loss is significant every year in symptomatic vines, and apoplexy cases result in one hundred percent loss for the affected vine. The global economic cost of Esca is estimated in the billions of dollars annually. Mature vineyards older than fifteen years typically show incidence rates of five to thirty percent, which can reach fifty percent or more in severely affected blocks.

Within an individual vine, the fungi spread slowly through woody tissue over months to years. External symptoms may not appear for three to ten years after initial infection, meaning a vine can be diseased long before any visible sign is present. Between vines, spores spread at pruning time through wind and contaminated tools. The chronic disease form is very slow, developing over many seasons. The apoplectic form, in contrast, is extremely sudden once triggered by heat stress, with complete vine collapse occurring within two to five days.

Esca spreads primarily through fresh pruning wounds. When cuts are made in the vine, the exposed wood surface is colonized by airborne spores within hours. These spores are produced on infected wood in the vineyard and travel on wind to land on freshly cut surfaces. Contaminated pruning tools are an equally important vector. When an infected vine is cut and the same unsterilized tool is used on a healthy vine, the fungi are transferred directly in the sap on the blade. Infected nursery stock is a major source of new vineyard infections, as vines can carry the pathogen internally without showing any symptoms at planting. Esca does not spread through soil, through root contact, or through irrigation water. Once inside the vine, the fungi grow progressively through the woody tissue toward the roots over many seasons.

Esca is caused by three main fungal species acting together as a complex. Phaeomoniella chlamydospora is an early colonizer that causes brown and black staining of the vascular tissue. Phaeoacremonium minimum, formerly known as P. aleophilum, is a vascular pathogen that causes the characteristic esca proper. Fomitiporia mediterranea is a basidiomycete that causes white spongy rot in the older central wood. These fungi produce spores under wet conditions that are released and carried by wind to pruning wounds. They survive for extended periods inside woody tissue and in infected pruning stubs. Their slow growth through wood explains the long latency period between infection and symptom expression.

On leaves, Esca produces interveinal chlorosis with reddish-brown necrotic spots that create the distinctive tiger stripe pattern. Leaf margins may dry, brown, and curl inward. These symptoms typically appear suddenly in mid-summer and may vary year to year, with symptomatic vines sometimes appearing healthy the following season.

On berries, small dark purple to black spots appear on the berry surface with lighter halos. These spots are the black measles for which the disease is named. Affected berries crack and shrivel at the spots, making entire clusters unmarketable.

In the wood, cutting across the trunk reveals the most reliable diagnostic signs. Brown or black staining in the vascular tissue and white, soft, spongy rot in the older central wood confirm Esca. The infected wood has a characteristic dirty or decayed texture and may show gummosis at pruning wounds.

Apoplexy presents as sudden wilting and death of all shoots on one or more arms, or the entire vine, within two to five days. The foliage collapses as if from severe drought but does not recover when irrigated. Cutting the trunk confirms infection through the dark internal staining.

Esca is primarily a disease of mature vines. Vines under five years old are rarely affected. Older vines become progressively more susceptible as their woody structure accumulates pruning wounds and internal decay. Infection occurs at pruning time, particularly when wounds are exposed to rain or wet conditions shortly after cutting. Heat waves in summer trigger apoplexy in vines that already carry substantial internal fungal load. Drought, heat, and nutrient deficiency worsen the probability and severity of apoplexy. The disease is present worldwide but is worst in warm, Mediterranean, and semi-arid climates.

The cycle begins when airborne spores from infected wood or nursery stock land on fresh pruning wounds. The fungi colonize the wound surface and grow into the woody tissue progressively over months and years. The internal wood decays slowly while the vine continues to function. Eventually the fungal load disrupts vascular function enough to produce external symptoms. In summers with extreme heat, the infected vascular system fails catastrophically, causing apoplexy. Infected pruning stubs in the vineyard continuously produce spores during wet weather throughout the year, infecting adjacent vines season after season.

Because there is no cure once the wood is infected, all management is preventive. The most important practice is protecting pruning wounds immediately after every cut. Wound protectants including thiophanate-methyl fungicide paint and formulations containing Trichoderma harzianum should be applied to every cut surface within hours of pruning. These products colonize the wound and exclude the Esca pathogens before they can establish. Pruning tools must be sterilized between every vine using ten percent bleach solution or seventy percent alcohol. Carrying two pairs of shears and alternating between them while the other soaks in sterilant is a practical method. Pruning during dry weather reduces spore release. Pruning during the coldest part of winter, when fungal activity is lowest, reduces infection risk. Double pruning, which involves a rough cut in autumn followed by the final cut in late winter, minimizes the time wounds are exposed during high-risk periods.

New vineyards should be established only with certified disease-free planting material. Every vine should be inspected at the graft union and a cross-section of the wood should be examined for dark staining before accepting any nursery material.

When infection is discovered early, surgical interventions can extend vine life. Trunk renewal removes the infected trunk entirely and trains a new shoot from the rootstock base. This is effective when disease has not yet reached the roots and typically requires three to five years to restore full production. Curettage involves surgically carving out infected wood from the trunk and sealing the wound with a biological protectant. Removing individual infected arms while the trunk remains is feasible for early or localized infections.

No systemic fungicide eliminates established infections inside the wood. Chemical treatments are effective only as wound protectants applied immediately at the time of pruning.

When about to prune, prepare sterilization solution before starting and apply wound protectant to every cut immediately after making it. When tiger-stripe leaf symptoms appear mid-summer, mark affected vines with flagging tape, assess the number of affected vines, and plan trunk renewal for vines that have shown symptoms for two or more consecutive years. When a vine collapses suddenly, cut the trunk to confirm internal staining, remove the vine, and evaluate the surrounding vines for early symptoms. When planting a new vineyard, inspect every vine's wood cross-section before accepting the material and refuse any showing dark staining.

Survey the entire vineyard annually in mid-summer, which is July or August in the northern hemisphere, when foliar symptoms are most visible. Record all symptomatic vines by location using GPS or a vineyard map. Track the progression of each marked vine year over year to identify those worsening consistently. During dormant pruning, cross-section flagged vines to confirm internal staining and assess how deep the decay has progressed. Track apoplexy events during summer heat waves as a measure of the disease's severity in the block.

Esca is present in all grape-growing regions worldwide. It is most economically damaging in France, Italy, Spain, Portugal, California, and Australia. Incidence in mature vineyards typically ranges from five to thirty percent and can exceed fifty percent in the most affected blocks. The disease became significantly more prevalent in European vineyards after sodium arsenite, a highly effective wood protectant, was banned in 2002.

Can I cure a vine that already has Esca? No. Once the wood is infected, no chemical or biological treatment eliminates the fungi from inside the tissue. Management focuses on slowing progression through surgical interventions and protecting healthy vines.

My vine suddenly wilted and died in three days. Is this Esca? This sounds like apoplexy, the acute form of Esca triggered by heat stress in an already-infected vine. Cut the trunk. If you see brown or black wood staining, Esca is confirmed.

How do I know if my pruning tools are spreading the disease? You cannot tell visually. Assume contamination and sterilize between every vine, especially in vineyards with known Esca history.

My vineyard is new. Can I get Esca? Yes, through infected nursery stock. This is why certified disease-free planting material is essential. Always inspect wood cross-sections when accepting new vines.

Does Esca spread from vine to vine through the soil? Not typically. The main spread is through pruning wounds via airborne spores and contaminated tools, not through soil or root contact.

Grape Downy Mildew is one of the most destructive diseases in viticulture worldwide, caused by Plasmopara viticola, an oomycete or water mold rather than a true fungus. This distinction is critical because it means standard fungicides effective against true fungi do not work against this pathogen, and only products specifically labeled for oomycete diseases can provide control. The disease creates pale yellow oil spots on the upper surface of leaves and a characteristic white, downy growth on the underside. It attacks leaves, shoots, tendrils, and fruit clusters with devastating speed during warm, rainy weather. A single warm, wet night can initiate a massive infection event across an entire vineyard. European wine grape varieties are extremely susceptible, while American varieties carry natural partial resistance. Plasmopara viticola originated in North America and was accidentally introduced to Europe in the 1870s, nearly destroying the European wine industry before control measures were developed.

Grape Downy Mildew is rated as very high danger. Repeated severe defoliation weakens vines significantly, and young vines can be killed outright in their first season. Yield losses in unprotected susceptible varieties during wet seasons commonly reach fifty to one hundred percent. The economic impact is catastrophic when cluster infection occurs, because infected grapes are unmarketable and produce off-flavors in wine. The disease spreads so rapidly that by the time oil spots are visible, the vineyard may already be heavily infected beyond what can be seen. Once established, systemic fungicides provide only limited curative activity of twenty-four to seventy-two hours, making preventive protection essential.

The incubation period ranges from five to eighteen days, with shorter periods in warm weather around twenty degrees Celsius and longer periods in cool conditions. During a rain event, sporangia released from sporulating lesions disperse to the entire vineyard within hours through wind and rain. From the first oil spots to complete canopy infection takes only one to three weeks in warm, wet conditions. This makes Downy Mildew one of the fastest-spreading diseases in all of viticulture.

The primary overwintering source is oospores, which are sexually produced thick-walled spores that survive in fallen infected leaves and in the soil. In spring, oospores germinate after rain and release zoospores that swim through water films on leaf surfaces to stomata, where they initiate primary infections. The trigger point for primary infection risk is captured in the ten-ten-ten rule: the disease begins when shoots reach ten centimeters, air temperature exceeds ten degrees Celsius, and ten millimeters of rain have fallen in the previous twenty-four hours. Once primary infections establish, the white downy growth on leaf undersides produces sporangia that contain more zoospores. Rain and wind disperse these sporangia across the entire vineyard. Cluster infection is most devastating from flowering through berry set, when infected flowers and young berries turn brown and die. Long-distance introduction of the pathogen occurs through infected planting material brought to new vineyards.

Plasmopara viticola is an oomycete, a group of organisms more closely related to algae and brown algae than to true fungi. This evolutionary distance explains why fungicides that work by targeting fungal-specific biochemical pathways are ineffective against it. The pathogen produces two types of asexual spores: macrosporangia that are dispersed by wind and rain, and zoospores released from the macrosporangia in the presence of water that swim through water films to reach stomata. The sexual stage produces oospores through the mating of compatible strains, and these oospores provide the long-term survival in soil across seasons. The pathogen has shown the ability to evolve resistance to phenylamide fungicides such as metalaxyl, and this resistance is now widespread globally.

On the upper leaf surface, Downy Mildew produces pale yellow to lime-green oil spots that appear translucent and greasy when held to light. These spots are roughly circular but are sometimes limited by leaf veins into angular shapes. Multiple spots coalesce to cover large areas of the leaf, which eventually turn brown and die.

On the lower leaf surface, directly beneath the oil spots, a white, cottony, fluffy growth appears. This is the sporangial growth, the production of infectious spore packets, and it is the primary diagnostic sign of active Downy Mildew. The color of this growth can range from pure white in early season infections to grayish or brownish as oospores form later in the season.

On shoots and tendrils, brown to black lesions form and shoot tips may die and curl into a shepherd's crook shape. Infected tendrils fail to grip and lose their structural function.

On clusters, infected flower clusters turn brown and die before they can set fruit, which is the most devastating outcome. Young infected berries turn gray-brown in a condition called grey rot or leather rot, then harden and shrivel. Late-season berry infections can show white sporulation under humid conditions.

Downy Mildew develops in temperatures between twelve and thirty degrees Celsius with an optimum between twenty and twenty-five degrees. Rain is required for oospore germination, for sporangia dispersal, and for zoospore release and movement to stomata. Overnight dew or rain provides the leaf wetness that enables infection. The disease is most damaging from flowering through veraison, the point of color change in berries. European wine grape varieties including Chardonnay, Merlot, Cabernet Sauvignon, and Riesling have essentially no meaningful resistance and are fully susceptible.

Oospores overwinter in soil and infected leaf litter. Spring rains trigger oospore germination, producing macrosporangia that release zoospores capable of infecting leaves through stomata. Primary infection begins when the ten-ten-ten rule conditions are met. After an incubation of five to eighteen days, oil spots appear and white sporangial growth develops on the leaf underside. Rain and wind disperse new sporangia across the vineyard. Multiple infection cycles occur throughout the season during warm, wet weather. At season end, sexual reproduction between compatible strains produces oospores within infected tissue, which fall with leaves to the soil and survive until the following spring.

Canopy management is the most important cultural practice. Leaf removal and shoot positioning improve air circulation and reduce humidity around clusters, significantly lowering infection risk. Removing leaves around clusters at bloom directly reduces the risk of the most economically damaging cluster infection. Overhead irrigation should be avoided. Leaf litter should be shredded and incorporated at season end to reduce oospore survival. Rows should be oriented to maximize sunlight and airflow.

Variety choice is the most powerful long-term tool. American species and PIWI varieties, which are European-American hybrids bred for disease resistance, carry significant natural resistance. PIWI varieties such as Regent, Johanniter, Bronner, Solaris, Cabernet Blanc, and Soreli can reduce fungicide requirements by fifty to eighty percent compared to susceptible European varieties. Pure Vitis vinifera varieties have no meaningful resistance and require full chemical protection programs.

Chemical control must use only products specifically labeled for downy mildew and active against oomycetes, because products designed for true fungal diseases will not work. Protectant fungicides including mancozeb, folpet, and copper coat the leaf surface and prevent infection but provide no curative activity. They must be reapplied after significant rain. Systemic fungicides provide penetration into leaf tissue and some curative activity. Phenylamide products such as metalaxyl and mefenoxam have systemic action but resistance is widespread and they must never be used alone, always in premix with a protectant. Carboxylic acid amide products such as dimethomorph, iprovalicarb, and mandipropamid are systemic with excellent anti-sporulation activity and some curative action up to forty-eight hours after infection. Cymoxanil provides curative activity up to twenty-four hours post-infection. The spray program should begin when shoots reach ten centimeters or when the ten-ten-ten trigger is met, whichever comes first. Intervals are seven to ten days in wet weather and fourteen days in dry periods. Systemic fungicides should be prioritized at bloom for maximum protection of flowers. Metalaxyl and mefenoxam should be rotated with CAA products and cymoxanil-containing products, and should always be used only in premix formulations to manage resistance risk.

In organic viticulture, copper-based fungicides are the primary tool and can be effective protectants when applied on schedule. Potassium phosphite triggers plant defense responses and provides limited but real suppression and is approved in some organic programs. Bacillus subtilis has limited suppressive activity and plays a supportive role. Canopy management and PIWI variety selection are the most impactful organic strategies.

When shoots reach ten centimeters in a wet spring, begin the protectant fungicide program immediately without waiting for symptoms. When the ten-ten-ten rule is triggered, apply a systemic plus protectant tank mix before the next rain event. When oil spots become visible on leaves, apply a systemic fungicide immediately and increase spray frequency to every five to seven days. At the beginning of bloom, apply a systemic fungicide regardless of weather because this is the single most critical spray timing to protect flowers. When white sporulation is visible on lesions, indicating an active epidemic, spray every five to seven days and remove infected shoots.

Monitor shoots daily once they reach five centimeters because the ten-ten-ten trigger can arrive suddenly. Check both leaf surfaces at every scouting visit, looking for oil spots on the upper surface and white sporulation on the lower surface. Pay special attention to the youngest shoots and cluster zones. Track sporulation intensity because sporulating lesions indicate active epidemic spread and require an immediate response. After any rain event meeting the ten-ten-ten conditions, scout for oil spots five to ten days later. Regional disease forecasting systems can help optimize spray timing where available.

Downy Mildew originated in North America and was introduced to Europe in 1878 with near-catastrophic consequences for the wine industry. Today it is present in all grape-growing regions worldwide. It is most severe in humid continental and maritime climates such as France, Germany, Italy, the eastern United States, and China. In dry Mediterranean climates it causes less pressure but can still produce severe losses in wet years. It is also damaging in South America, South Africa, and Australia.

I see white growth on the underside of leaves. Is that Downy Mildew or Powdery Mildew? White downy growth on the underside of leaves beneath yellow oil spots is Downy Mildew. White powdery growth on the upper surface of leaves is Powdery Mildew. They require completely different fungicides, so correct identification is essential.

Can I use the same fungicides for both diseases? Not necessarily. Downy Mildew requires oomycete-specific products such as mancozeb, copper, metalaxyl, cymoxanil, and mandipropamid. Powdery Mildew requires DMIs, strobilurins, or sulfur. Some premix products cover both. Always read the label.

The weather was dry for two weeks and disease still appeared. How? Oospores in the soil germinate with the first significant rain, even after a long dry period. One overnight rain after weeks of dry weather can trigger a primary infection event if the ten-ten-ten conditions are otherwise met.

Metalaxyl stopped working in my vineyard. What happened? Metalaxyl resistance in Plasmopara viticola is widespread globally. Never use metalaxyl alone. Always use it in premix with mancozeb or copper and rotate with cymoxanil, mandipropamid, or dimethomorph.

Can I make wine from grapes affected by Downy Mildew? Grapes with leather rot produce off-flavors and abnormal fermentation. Heavily infected lots should not be vinified. Lightly affected clusters can sometimes be processed after removing diseased portions.

Potato Late Blight is the most dangerous potato disease in the world, caused by Phytophthora infestans, an oomycete or water mold rather than a true fungus. It is historically responsible for the Irish Famine of 1845 to 1852, during which it destroyed the potato crop across Ireland causing over one million deaths and the emigration of two million more. Today it remains an active and serious threat, costing global agriculture more than six billion dollars annually. The disease creates large, dark, water-soaked spots on leaves with white cottony mold underneath, and causes reddish-brown rot inside tubers that can spread through an entire storage facility. It spreads with extreme speed, capable of destroying an entire field in one to two weeks and traveling by wind for kilometers to infect neighboring farms. Because it is an oomycete and not a true fungus, most standard fungicides have no effect on it, and only oomycete-active products can provide control.

Late Blight is rated as critical, the highest possible danger level for any plant disease. It can kill entire plants and entire fields within seven to fourteen days under favorable conditions. Yield losses can reach one hundred percent in the field, with additional storage losses when infected tubers are mixed with healthy ones. The speed of destruction is extreme, making it unlike any other common crop disease. Any confirmed sighting in the field should be treated as an immediate emergency.

The incubation period is just two to four days under cool, wet conditions, one of the shortest of any major crop disease. An entire field can collapse within seven to fourteen days of first infection under favorable weather. Spores travel by wind for kilometers, meaning a single infected field can endanger every potato farm in the surrounding region. Infected tubers spread disease to healthy tubers in storage, and an entire storage bin can be lost in weeks from a single infected lot.

The primary overwintering source is infected potato tubers, whether left in the field as volunteers, stored in facilities, or discarded in cull piles near the field. These infected tubers begin sporulating under cool, wet spring conditions and release spores that are carried by wind. Rain releases and disperses sporangia from infected plant material. Zoospores released from sporangia in the presence of water infect tubers through lenticels and wounds when sporangia are washed into the soil by rain. In storage, infected tubers produce mycelium that spreads to adjacent healthy tubers, and secondary bacterial soft rots rapidly accelerate the destruction. Cull piles of discarded infected potatoes left near fields are a major and often overlooked inoculum source that should always be destroyed immediately.

Phytophthora infestans is an oomycete, biologically more similar to algae and diatoms than to true fungi. This evolutionary distance is why fungicides targeting fungal-specific processes such as ergosterol biosynthesis have no activity against it. Only oomycete-specific products work. The pathogen produces sporangia that can either germinate directly at cool temperatures or release biflagellate zoospores at warmer temperatures, giving it flexibility across a range of conditions. Where both A1 and A2 mating types are present, sexual reproduction produces oospores that survive in soil for multiple years, complicating long-term management. Metalaxyl and mefenoxam resistance is extremely common worldwide and must be assumed unless specifically proven otherwise in a given region. The pathogen continues to evolve, and new genetic lineages emerge regularly that can overcome host resistance genes.

On leaves, Late Blight produces large, irregular, water-soaked lesions that begin pale green and rapidly transition to dark brown or black necrosis with an irregular pale yellow border. In humid conditions, white cottony sporulation appears on the underside of infected leaves, and this is the key diagnostic sign requiring immediate action. Lesions expand visibly day by day and the entire plant can collapse within three to five days. A distinct musty or earthy odor is often noticed in heavily infected fields.

On stems, dark brown to black lesions progress rapidly and cause complete stem death, collapsing the entire shoot above the infection point.

On tubers, the disease produces reddish-brown discoloration beginning just beneath the skin, extending inward as a firm, dry rot with an irregular pattern. This is distinct from bacterial soft rots, which are watery and strongly odorous. Infected tubers in storage attract secondary bacterial invasion and can liquefy entirely. One infected tuber in a storage bin can spread disease to the entire lot.

Late Blight thrives in temperatures between ten and twenty-five degrees Celsius with an optimum around eighteen degrees. It requires greater than ninety percent relative humidity for at least twelve hours to produce spores effectively. Cool nights with dew and frequent rainfall are the classic warning conditions. Forecasting systems including the Hutton Criteria, which define infection risk as two consecutive days with minimum temperatures above ten degrees and relative humidity above ninety percent for at least six hours, are used by growers to anticipate outbreaks before symptoms appear.

Primary inoculum comes from infected tubers overwintering in soil as volunteers, from unharvested cull piles, and from storage facilities. These sources begin sporulating under cool, wet spring conditions. Sporangia are dispersed by wind and rain to potato foliage. Zoospores or directly germinating sporangia infect through stomata and wounds. After an incubation of just two to four days, the plant begins sporulating itself, amplifying the inoculum exponentially. Rain washes sporangia into soil where zoospores infect tubers through lenticels. Infected tubers at harvest carry disease into storage, where it spreads to healthy lots. Where oospores form from sexual reproduction, they survive in soil for years and complicate rotation-based management.

Planting only certified disease-free seed potatoes is the most fundamental preventive measure and is not optional. Infected seed is the most direct way to introduce the disease into a clean field. Resistant varieties with multiple resistance genes, particularly those with quantitative multigenic resistance, significantly reduce epidemic severity and are available through regional variety programs. Even resistant varieties require some fungicide protection in high-pressure seasons. Volunteer potato plants in and around fields serve as green bridges for the pathogen and must be destroyed. Cull piles must be buried deeply or composted far from fields immediately after sorting. Harvest should occur only when haulm is fully dead and tuber skins are fully set to minimize tuber infection during harvest operations.

Chemical control must use only oomycete-active fungicides. Protectant fungicides including chlorothalonil, mancozeb, and copper coat the tissue surface and prevent infection but have no curative activity. They must be reapplied frequently, especially after rain. Systemic fungicides with curative activity include dimethomorph, fluopicolide, mandipropamid, cymoxanil, and propamocarb. These penetrate tissue and can stop infections in progress for a limited window of twenty-four to forty-eight hours. Metalaxyl and mefenoxam are systemic but resistance is extremely common worldwide and they should never be used as solo products, only in premix with a contact fungicide. The spray program should begin preventively using forecasting criteria, not after symptoms appear. During high-risk weather, applications every five to seven days are required. An example rotation would cycle through mancozeb alone, then mandipropamid plus mancozeb, then chlorothalonil alone, then cymoxanil plus mancozeb, then fluopicolide plus propamocarb, repeating without ever using the same mode of action in consecutive applications.

Storage management is as important as field management. All tubers should be carefully inspected before storage. Only healthy, dry, properly cured tubers should enter storage. Curing at twelve to fifteen degrees Celsius for ten to fourteen days allows wound healing before final storage temperature. Storage should be maintained at four to seven degrees Celsius with good ventilation and humidity around ninety percent. Regular inspection of stored lots is essential and any showing rot must be removed immediately before it spreads.

In organic programs, copper-based fungicides are the primary chemical tool. Potassium phosphite provides systemic activity and some curative effect and is approved in some organic programs. Resistant varieties and extremely tight copper spray schedules of five to seven days during wet weather are the foundation of organic Late Blight management.

When disease is reported in the surrounding region, spray immediately without waiting for symptoms in your own field. When cool, foggy, or rainy weather is forecast, apply a systemic fungicide twenty-four hours before the weather event. When white mold appears on leaf undersides, this is an emergency requiring action within hours, not days. Remove all visibly infected plants, spray the entire field with a systemic plus protectant combination, and stop all overhead irrigation immediately. Alert neighboring farms because spores travel kilometers by wind. At harvest, inspect all tubers carefully and do not store any showing infection.

Scout fields twice weekly from canopy closure through harvest during high-risk seasons. Focus first on field edges, low-lying areas, and areas near irrigation sources or volunteer plants. Check leaf undersides carefully because white sporulation appears there before upper surface symptoms are obvious. Use weather monitoring and disease forecasting tools to predict risk periods. Register for regional Late Blight alerts through local agricultural extension or plant health services. After any confirmed case is reported in the region, increase scouting to daily inspections.

Late Blight is present on every continent where potatoes are grown. It is most severe in cool, wet climates including Ireland, the United Kingdom, northern Europe, the Pacific Northwest of the United States, the Andes region where the pathogen originated, and highland Africa. New, more aggressive genetic lineages including US-8 and several EU lineages have spread globally since the 1990s and have complicated resistance management in many regions.

How do I tell Late Blight from Early Blight on potato? Late Blight produces irregular water-soaked spots with white mold on the leaf underside, spreads in days, and can collapse a whole field in one to two weeks. Early Blight produces bullseye circular spots with rings, starts on old lower leaves, spreads over weeks, and has no white mold. Late Blight is always an emergency requiring immediate action.

I used metalaxyl and it did not work. Why? Metalaxyl and mefenoxam resistance is extremely common worldwide. Switch immediately to mandipropamid, fluopicolide plus propamocarb, or dimethomorph, and never use metalaxyl alone.

My stored potatoes are rotting. Is this Late Blight? Possibly. Late Blight tubers show firm reddish-brown dry rot progressing into the flesh. If you also see watery soft rot and a strong odor, bacteria have entered through the Late Blight lesions. Remove all affected tubers immediately and improve ventilation.

Can Late Blight spread from potato to tomato? Yes. Phytophthora infestans infects both crops. If you grow both, an outbreak in one means the other is at immediate risk. Manage both simultaneously and keep them separated where possible.

How long does the pathogen survive in soil? As oospores from sexual reproduction it can survive for several years. Regular rotation, elimination of volunteers, and destruction of cull piles are essential for long-term management.

Isariopsis Leaf Spot, also known as Grape Leaf Blight, is a fungal disease of grapevines caused by Pseudocercospora vitis, previously known under the synonyms Isariopsis clavispora and Cercospora viticola. The disease primarily affects leaves, producing dark brown spots with a characteristic dark olive-colored sporulation on the underside that is its most reliable diagnostic feature. It causes progressive leaf yellowing and premature defoliation, weakening the vine as it enters winter dormancy. Isariopsis Leaf Spot is less aggressive and less economically important than Black Rot or Downy Mildew, but it can cause significant defoliation in warm, humid regions, particularly in tropical and subtropical grape-growing areas, and its impact accumulates over multiple seasons when management is neglected.

Isariopsis Leaf Spot is rated as moderate danger. It does not kill the grapevine and rarely causes direct fruit loss because berry infection is uncommon. Its primary harm is through defoliation, which reduces the vine's ability to produce and store carbohydrates before winter dormancy. Vines repeatedly defoliated by this disease in late summer and autumn enter winter with reduced energy reserves, lower cold hardiness, and diminished vigor for the following season. The economic impact is lower than Black Rot or Downy Mildew but meaningful in tropical and subtropical regions where the disease is most prevalent.

The incubation period from infection to visible symptoms is fourteen to twenty-one days. The disease builds slowly through summer, reaching its peak intensity in late summer and autumn. Significant defoliation, when it occurs, develops over four to eight weeks. The progression is gradual compared to more aggressive diseases, which means that well-timed management interventions can effectively control the season's impact.

Isariopsis Leaf Spot overwinters in infected fallen leaves on the vineyard floor, where the fungus survives as mycelium and conidia in the debris. In spring and early summer, warming temperatures and wet conditions stimulate spore production on this overwintered debris. Conidia are dispersed by wind and rain splash to the undersides of leaves, where they penetrate through stomata during periods of high humidity. Secondary infections occur throughout the growing season as new spore production from established lesions continues. The disease does not spread through soil, through root contact, or through pruning tools, unlike Esca. Infected leaves falling to the vineyard floor at the end of the season replenish the inoculum source for the following year.

Pseudocercospora vitis is an ascomycete fungus that survives as mycelium and conidia in infected fallen leaf debris on the vineyard floor. It produces long, multiseptate conidia in olive-colored clusters called fascicles on the underside of infected leaves. The conidia are produced most abundantly during periods of high relative humidity and do not require rain for their formation, only humid air. Penetration occurs exclusively through stomata on the leaf underside. The fungus does not persist long-term in soil and is entirely dependent on leaf litter for overwintering. This dependency on leaf debris makes litter removal the most powerful single management intervention available.

On leaves, Isariopsis Leaf Spot produces irregular, angular to rounded spots between two and fifteen millimeters in diameter. On the upper leaf surface the spots appear dark brown to reddish-brown with diffuse, sometimes unclear margins. On the lower leaf surface, directly beneath the spots, dark olivaceous sporulation appears, ranging from olive-green to black in color. This dark olive underside growth is the key diagnostic feature that distinguishes this disease from Downy Mildew, which produces white sporulation on the leaf underside. Affected lesions may coalesce into large blighted areas. Infected leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely, and defoliation can be substantial in severe cases during late summer and autumn.

On petioles, dark elongated lesions can appear, occasionally causing premature leaf drop before the leaf itself shows complete symptoms.

On berries, small dark spots occasionally form on the berry surface near harvest, but berry infection is uncommon and rarely causes significant direct fruit loss.

Isariopsis Leaf Spot thrives in temperatures between twenty and thirty degrees Celsius and under conditions of high relative humidity with frequent rainfall. Dense canopies with poor air circulation create the microclimate most favorable to the disease by trapping humidity near the leaf surface. The disease is most severe in late summer and autumn and is most prevalent in tropical and subtropical grape-growing regions where humidity remains high throughout the season.

The fungus survives winter in infected leaf debris on the vineyard floor. When warm, moist conditions return in spring and early summer, the pathogen produces conidia on the debris that are dispersed by wind and rain splash to leaf undersides. Infection occurs through stomata during humid periods. After an incubation of fourteen to twenty-one days, symptoms develop and new conidia are produced on the underside of infected leaves. Secondary infections continue throughout the growing season. As the season advances into late summer and autumn, the disease intensifies and defoliation accelerates. Infected leaves fall to the ground and replenish the leaf litter inoculum pool for the following season.

Removing and destroying fallen infected leaves after harvest is the single most effective management action for Isariopsis Leaf Spot. Because the disease depends entirely on leaf litter for overwintering, thorough leaf removal from the vineyard floor eliminates the primary inoculum source and directly reduces next season's disease pressure.
