Improving canopy airflow through shoot positioning, leaf removal, and appropriate training systems reduces the humidity microclimate that favors the disease. Drip or subsurface irrigation should be used in preference to overhead systems. Balanced fertilization with avoidance of excess nitrogen keeps canopy density manageable.

This disease is usually managed as a secondary benefit of fungicide programs targeting the more serious diseases of Black Rot and Downy Mildew, because the fungicides used for those diseases, including copper, mancozeb, captan, azoxystrobin, and tebuconazole, are also effective against Pseudocercospora vitis. When Isariopsis Leaf Spot requires a dedicated chemical program, applications of copper or mancozeb every ten to fourteen days during wet periods provide adequate protection. Strobilurins and triazoles can be added when additional systemic activity is needed. Modes of action should be rotated to prevent resistance.

In organic programs, copper-based fungicides applied preventively are the most reliable option. The emphasis should be on leaf litter removal and canopy management as the primary organic tools.

In early summer when no symptoms are yet visible, remove leaf litter from the previous season, improve canopy airflow, and apply a preventive copper spray if wet weather is forecast. When first spots appear on lower leaves, spray fungicide and remove heavily infected leaves. When significant defoliation is occurring, spray immediately and remove fallen leaves from the vineyard floor to reduce active inoculum. After harvest, collect and destroy all fallen leaves, because this is the intervention that most directly reduces the following year's disease pressure.

Begin scouting in mid-summer when the disease typically starts to appear. Focus on lower canopy leaves where the disease initiates. Examine leaf undersides with a hand lens, looking for dark olive-colored conidiophore clusters that confirm Pseudocercospora vitis rather than another disease. Record the percentage of leaves with symptoms and estimate the percentage defoliation occurring before harvest. Monitor more intensively in humid, low-lying areas of the vineyard where air drainage is poorest.

Isariopsis Leaf Spot occurs worldwide wherever grapevines are grown, but is most economically significant in Southeast Asia, South America, and parts of southern and eastern Europe. It is particularly prevalent in India, China, Japan, Brazil, and Argentina. In dry Mediterranean climates it causes less pressure, but warm, wet years can produce significant outbreaks even in drier regions.

How serious is this disease compared to other grape diseases? It is less serious than Black Rot or Downy Mildew. It mainly causes late-season defoliation rather than direct fruit loss. However, repeated severe defoliation weakens vines over time and reduces winter hardiness.

How do I tell this apart from Downy Mildew? Both cause spots on leaves with underside growth, but the color of that growth is the definitive difference. Isariopsis Leaf Spot has dark olive or brown sporulation on the leaf underside. Downy Mildew has white, fluffy, cottony sporulation. If the underside growth is olive-colored, it is Isariopsis.

My leaves are falling off late in the season. Is this normal or a problem? Some late-season leaf drop is normal senescence. If leaves are dropping with dark spots and olive sporulation on the underside, it is Isariopsis Leaf Spot. Defoliation occurring before harvest is a problem because it reduces fruit quality, ripening, and winter hardiness.

What is the most important thing I can do to prevent it? Remove and destroy fallen infected leaves after harvest. This eliminates the overwintering inoculum and significantly reduces the following season's disease pressure.

Can Isariopsis spread from grapes to other crops? Pseudocercospora vitis is specific to grapevines and does not infect other common agricultural crops. Wild Vitis species in nearby hedgerows or forests can serve as alternative hosts and potential inoculum sources.

Leaf Mold is a fungal disease of tomato caused by Passalora fulva, formerly known as Cladosporium fulvum and also referred to as Fulvia fulva in some literature. It is primarily a disease of protected tomato production, most commonly occurring in greenhouses and high tunnels where humidity can rise and remain above the critical threshold of eighty-five percent relative humidity. Under those conditions the disease spreads rapidly and can strip all leaves from greenhouse plants within two to four weeks. In open-field production it is less common but can occur in dense plantings during prolonged wet and cloudy weather. The defining characteristic of the disease is an olive-green to grayish-purple velvety mold that forms on the underside of infected leaves, directly beneath pale yellow spots visible on the upper surface.

Leaf Mold is rated as moderate danger in open fields and high danger in greenhouses and high tunnels. It does not kill plants directly, but complete defoliation severely weakens them and eliminates yield by removing the photosynthetic surface needed to fill fruit. Fruit infection is rare, making defoliation the primary economic concern. The disease is one of the leading causes of losses in protected tomato production worldwide and requires consistent humidity management to keep under control.

The incubation period from infection to visible symptoms is ten to fourteen days. In a greenhouse where humidity is consistently above eighty-five percent and ventilation is poor, the disease can spread through the entire structure in two to four weeks. In open-field conditions the spread is slower and more dependent on weather events. An important characteristic of the pathogen is that its conidia can survive on greenhouse structures including frames, plastic covering, stakes, and trellises for months to years between crop cycles, making them a persistent inoculum source for every new crop planted into an inadequately sanitized structure.

The primary source in greenhouse production is infected plant debris and contaminated greenhouse surfaces where conidia survive between crops. At the start of each new crop cycle, humidity rises and dormant spores on structures become active and are carried by internal air currents from plant to plant. Workers handling infected plants transfer conidia on their hands, clothing, and tools, which is a significant pathway of spread especially in the early stages of an outbreak before the problem is recognized. Conidia germinate on the leaf underside and penetrate through stomata. The critical requirement is relative humidity above eighty-five percent. Below this threshold, conidia cannot germinate and the disease stops spreading regardless of how much inoculum is present. This single environmental threshold is the most important principle in managing Leaf Mold.

Passalora fulva is an ascomycete fungus with high race diversity, with over thirty races identified based on their ability to overcome specific resistance genes in tomato called Cf genes. The sexual stage is rarely observed, and the disease is propagated almost entirely through asexual conidia. These conidia are produced in chains on conidiophores that emerge from stomata on the leaf underside, creating the visible velvety sporulation. The spores are highly resilient and remain viable on surfaces including metal, plastic, and wood for extended periods between crops. The fungus requires relative humidity above eighty-five percent both for spore germination and for new sporulation, which is why humidity control is simultaneously the most important monitoring parameter and the most effective management tool.

On the upper leaf surface, Leaf Mold produces pale green to yellow spots of variable size with diffuse, irregular margins. These spots may be confused with nutrient deficiencies or other diseases unless the leaf underside is examined.

On the lower leaf surface, directly beneath the upper surface spots, olive-green to grayish-purple velvety mold appears. This sporulation is the defining diagnostic feature of Leaf Mold. The color can range from olive-green early in the season to gray-brown as the season progresses, and can appear purplish under some conditions. This growth is the conidiophore production generating new infectious spores.

Infected leaves curl at the edges, turn brown, and drop progressively from the lower to the upper canopy. In severe outbreaks, complete defoliation occurs within weeks.

Stem and fruit infection are rare and only occur in very severe cases under extremely high humidity.

Relative humidity above eighty-five percent is the critical condition for Leaf Mold, both for spore germination and for new sporulation. Temperature between sixteen and twenty-four degrees Celsius, with an optimum around twenty-two degrees, is the most favorable range. Poor air circulation within a greenhouse creates local pockets of very high humidity that enable rapid spread even when average greenhouse humidity is somewhat lower. Overhead or foliar irrigation contributes directly to humidity and leaf wetness and accelerates the disease significantly.

Conidia survive on greenhouse structures, in plant debris, and in soil between crop cycles. When a new crop is planted and canopy density increases, humidity rises above eighty-five percent and dormant conidia are activated. Air currents distribute spores through the greenhouse to leaf undersides. Conidia germinate and penetrate stomata during humid periods. After ten to fourteen days, new velvety sporulation appears on infected leaves and produces fresh conidia that air currents spread further. The cycle repeats rapidly as long as humidity remains above the threshold. At season end, conidia remain on greenhouse surfaces and in debris to initiate the cycle in the next crop.

Reducing relative humidity below eighty-five percent is the most important and most effective management action. This can be achieved by opening vents and running circulation fans, heating the greenhouse in the morning to reduce overnight condensation, reducing irrigation frequency, and avoiding all overhead or foliar watering. Below eighty-five percent relative humidity, sporulation stops and the disease ceases to spread regardless of inoculum levels, making environmental control more powerful than any chemical option.

Plant spacing should be adequate to allow airflow through the canopy. Infected leaves should be removed promptly and disposed of outside the greenhouse rather than composted inside. Workers should implement hygiene protocols including changing clothes and washing hands before entering high-value or clean sections.

Between crop cycles, thorough sanitization of the entire greenhouse is essential. All plant material must be completely removed. Surfaces including frames, plastic, stakes, trellises, and irrigation equipment should be washed with detergent to remove organic matter, then treated with bleach at one to ten dilution or quaternary ammonium compounds, and allowed to dry completely for at least one week before the next crop is transplanted.

Resistant varieties carrying Cf resistance genes are available and provide effective protection against specific races of the pathogen. However, resistance is race-specific and breaks down when a new race matching the variety's Cf gene is present. Combining resistant varieties with humidity management provides the most reliable protection.

Fungicide applications work significantly better when combined with humidity reduction and should not be relied upon as a substitute for environmental control. Effective fungicide classes include multi-site protectants such as chlorothalonil and mancozeb, strobilurins such as azoxystrobin, and triazoles such as difenoconazole. Applications every seven to ten days with thorough coverage of leaf undersides are required in active disease situations. Strobilurin resistance has been reported in some greenhouse populations, so modes of action should be rotated.

In organic programs, copper-based fungicides and Bacillus subtilis products provide some suppressive activity. Humidity management and thorough greenhouse sanitization between crops are the primary organic tools.

When humidity is consistently above eighty-five percent, open all vents, run circulation fans, and reduce irrigation frequency immediately because this is the priority action before any chemical response. When first olive mold appears on leaf undersides, remove infected leaves immediately, spray fungicide with thorough underside coverage, and urgently increase ventilation. When disease is spreading rapidly through a greenhouse, combine emergency ventilation with plant removal in the worst areas and fungicide application throughout. Between crops, sanitize all greenhouse structures, stakes, and equipment before the next planting.

Check plants two to three times per week in greenhouses throughout the growing season. Use a hygrometer to monitor humidity continuously and flag any periods when it consistently exceeds eighty-five percent. Scout during morning hours when sporulation is most visible. Check leaf undersides first because olive mold appears before upper surface yellowing becomes obvious. Record the percentage of plants affected and the severity of sporulation to track disease progression and the effectiveness of ventilation interventions.

Leaf Mold is primarily a disease of protected tomato production and is most economically significant in major greenhouse tomato regions including the Netherlands, the United Kingdom, Scandinavia, Spain, Italy, Turkey, Japan, South Korea, and greenhouse production areas of North America. Open-field occurrence is limited to warm, humid coastal environments where sustained humidity above eighty-five percent is possible.

I only see yellow spots on top of leaves. Is this Leaf Mold? Check the underside of the leaf. If there is an olive-green or gray velvety coating beneath the yellow area, it is Leaf Mold. Yellow spots on top with no mold underneath could be nutrient deficiency, mosaic virus, or another disease.

The velvety mold disappeared but yellow spots remain. Is the disease over? No. The mold disappears when humidity drops below eighty-five percent. The infection remains active inside the leaf tissue and sporulation will return immediately when humidity rises again.

Does Leaf Mold spread from my greenhouse to outdoor tomatoes? It can, but outdoor spread requires consistently high humidity. The disease is primarily a greenhouse problem. Outdoor outbreaks occur mainly in very dense plantings during prolonged wet weather.

My resistant variety still got Leaf Mold. Why? Cf gene resistance is race-specific. If a new race that overcomes your variety's Cf gene is present, resistance breaks down completely. Combining resistant varieties with humidity management is always more reliable than relying on genetics alone.

How do I sanitize my greenhouse between crops? Remove all plant material completely. Wash all surfaces with detergent solution to remove organic matter. Apply bleach at one to ten dilution or quaternary ammonium compounds to all surfaces. Allow to dry completely for at least one week before transplanting the next crop.

Septoria Leaf Spot is a fungal disease of tomato caused by Septoria lycopersici and is one of the most common tomato diseases in humid regions worldwide. It produces numerous small circular spots with gray centers and dark brown borders, and within each spot, tiny black dots are visible to the naked eye or under a hand lens. These black dots are pycnidia, the flask-shaped fruiting bodies of the fungus that release spores only when wet, and they are the definitive diagnostic feature that distinguishes Septoria from all other tomato leaf spot diseases. The disease begins on the oldest lower leaves after fruit set and moves steadily upward through the canopy. It rarely damages fruit directly, but severe defoliation exposes fruits to sunscald and weakens the plant's ability to fill them properly. Rain splash is the essential driver of spread, and keeping foliage dry through drip irrigation and mulching is highly effective at slowing the disease.

Septoria Leaf Spot is rated as moderate to high danger. It rarely kills plants but heavy defoliation causes significant indirect damage through sunscald on exposed fruit, reduced photosynthesis, and weakened fruit fill. Yield losses in severe, unmanaged cases range from twenty to forty percent. The disease is most damaging when it progresses rapidly into the upper canopy before or during fruit fill. It is consistently among the most common reasons for tomato defoliation in the eastern United States and other humid regions.

The incubation period from infection to visible symptoms is five to ten days. Progression through the canopy from lower to upper leaves occurs over four to eight weeks in typical seasons. The disease usually appears after fruit set and intensifies through harvest. The speed of progression is moderate compared to diseases like Late Blight or Bacterial Spot, but it is steady and cumulative without management intervention.

Septoria Leaf Spot overwinters in infected plant debris in the soil as pycnidia, which remain viable for two or more seasons. When rain or overhead irrigation wets the debris, pycnidia release spores that are splashed upward from the soil surface onto lower leaves. This rain splash mechanism is the primary driver of spread and the reason why mulching and drip irrigation are so effective at slowing the disease. Spores that land on leaves penetrate through stomata on the leaf underside during wet periods. Within the new lesions, pycnidia form rapidly and the cycle repeats with each rain event, progressively splashing spores higher into the canopy. Workers and equipment moving through wet fields spread spores laterally between plants and between fields. Infected transplants can also introduce the disease into clean fields.

Septoria lycopersici is an ascomycete fungus that survives as pycnidia in infected plant debris in soil. The pycnidia are flask-shaped structures embedded in lesion tissue that release spores called pycnidiospores, which are long, filiform, and multiseptate. These spores are released only when the pycnidia are wetted by rain or irrigation, which is why the activation of spread is directly tied to rain events. The sexual stage of the fungus is called Mycosphaerella lycopersici but is rarely observed in practice. The pathogen is primarily a tomato pathogen but is also reported on eggplant, pepino, and some solanaceous weeds. It does not affect wheat or other cereals despite sharing the Septoria genus name with wheat pathogens, which are entirely unrelated organisms.

On leaves, Septoria Leaf Spot produces numerous small, circular to slightly irregular spots between one and four millimeters in diameter. Each spot has a dark brown border surrounding a lighter gray or tan center. Inside the center of each spot, tiny black dots are visible. These are the pycnidia and are the definitive identification feature of Septoria Leaf Spot. The disease begins exclusively on lower, older leaves and moves upward through the canopy progressively over the season. In severe cases, spots coalesce into large irregular blighted areas and leaves turn yellow and drop. Defoliation can be complete on the lower and middle canopy in unmanaged crops.

On stems and petioles, similar small spots with dark borders can form. Stem infection is less common but possible in wet conditions. Petiole lesions may cause premature leaf drop.

On fruit, small spots with the same appearance as leaf lesions can rarely occur on green tomatoes. Fruit infection is uncommon and is not the main economic concern. The primary fruit-related problem is sunscald on exposed fruits after defoliation removes leaf cover.

Septoria Leaf Spot thrives in temperatures between twenty and twenty-five degrees Celsius. Extended wet periods with rain or overhead irrigation are essential for the splash-dispersal mechanism that drives spread. The disease is most severe after fruit set when plant tissue is aging and the canopy is dense. Overhead irrigation in warm weather produces ideal conditions for rapid progression through the canopy.

The cycle begins with pycnidia in infected plant debris overwintering in the soil. When rain or irrigation wets the debris, pycnidia release spores that are splashed onto lower leaves. Spores penetrate through stomata during wet periods. After five to ten days, new lesions form with new pycnidia inside them. Repeated rain events splash spores progressively higher into the canopy. Workers and tools spread spores laterally between plants. At season end, infected debris falls to the soil and pycnidia persist there for one to two seasons, ready to initiate the next season's epidemic.

Crop rotation away from tomato, eggplant, and solanaceous hosts for two or more seasons is the most effective long-term cultural tool because it allows pycnidia in the soil debris to degrade and substantially reduces the inoculum load for subsequent tomato crops. Organic mulch applied around the base of plants physically prevents rain splash from soil to lower leaves and is one of the most cost-effective early-season preventive measures available. Switching from overhead to drip irrigation eliminates the main driver of spore splash within the field and can dramatically slow disease progression on its own. Removing and destroying infected lower leaves as the disease progresses reduces the inoculum load in the canopy and improves airflow. Staking and training plants improves airflow through the canopy. Workers should avoid moving through fields when foliage is wet. All plant debris should be thoroughly removed and composted or buried after harvest.

Fungicide applications should begin when first symptoms appear or when weather consistently favors infection. Multi-site protectants including chlorothalonil and mancozeb provide no curative activity and must be applied preventively every seven to ten days. Copper-based products are effective and approved for organic use. Strobilurins such as azoxystrobin and pyraclostrobin provide protectant and some curative activity. Triazoles such as difenoconazole and propiconazole are systemic with curative activity within forty-eight to seventy-two hours of infection. SDHI products such as boscalid can be used in premix formulations. Applications every seven to ten days are typical, increasing to every five to seven days during wet weather. Thorough coverage of lower canopy leaves is important because this is where the disease initiates. Modes of action should be rotated with every two applications because strobilurin resistance in Septoria populations has been reported in some regions.

In organic programs, copper-based sprays are the most reliable option. Bacillus subtilis products have some suppressive activity and can support an integrated program. Mulching and drip irrigation remain the most impactful organic tools.

When spots appear on only the lowest two to three leaves, remove the affected leaves, apply mulch, and begin a fungicide spray program. When disease is progressing into the mid-canopy near the fruit zone, spray a systemic fungicide immediately and stop overhead irrigation. When significant defoliation has occurred with fruit exposed to direct sun, apply fungicide and consider shade cloth for sun protection of fruit. When overhead irrigation is currently in use, switch to drip immediately because this single change can dramatically slow disease spread.

Begin scouting at fruit set because the disease rarely appears before that stage. Focus on lower canopy leaves first because the disease always begins at the plant base. Use a hand lens at ten times magnification to confirm the tiny black pycnidia in lesion centers, which is the definitive identification feature. Record the percentage of plants affected, the highest leaf position showing disease, and the percentage of defoliation. Track disease progress weekly. Rapid upward movement signals the need for immediate intervention. Weather monitoring for periods of three or more consecutive days of temperatures between twenty and twenty-five degrees with rain represents high-risk windows requiring increased vigilance.

Septoria Leaf Spot is present wherever tomatoes are grown in humid conditions. It is most severe in the eastern United States, northern and eastern Europe, parts of Latin America and the Caribbean, and South and Southeast Asia. It is less common in arid or semi-arid climates with little summer rainfall.

How do I tell Septoria from Early Blight? Septoria produces many tiny spots between one and four millimeters with a gray center, a dark border, and tiny black dots inside the center. Early Blight produces fewer but larger spots between five and fifteen millimeters with concentric rings in a bullseye pattern and a yellow halo. The tiny black pycnidia inside Septoria lesions are the definitive distinguishing feature and are not present in Early Blight.

The fruits look fine but leaves are falling off. Should I worry? Yes. Defoliation exposes fruits to direct sunlight causing sunscald, which appears as white or tan bleached patches on fruit. It also weakens the plant's ability to fill and ripen fruit properly. Protecting the upper canopy is critical.

My plants get this every year. How do I break the cycle? Crop rotation is the key intervention. The fungus survives in debris for two or more seasons. Rotate out of tomatoes for at least two years and thoroughly remove all plant debris after each harvest.

Can I use neem oil or organic options effectively? Copper-based sprays are the most effective organic option. Neem oil and Bacillus subtilis have some suppressive effect but are less reliable. Timing, coverage, and frequency are critical with all organic options, and sprays should be applied every five to seven days during wet weather.

Is Septoria Leaf Spot on tomatoes the same as Septoria on wheat? No. Septoria leaf spot on wheat is caused by Zymoseptoria tritici, a completely different fungus. Despite sharing a common genus name in older taxonomy, these are unrelated pathogens and there is no cross-infection between tomato and wheat.

Spider mites are microscopic arachnids, not insects, belonging to the family Tetranychidae. The most economically important species on tomato is Tetranychus urticae, the two-spotted spider mite, named for two dark spots visible on the yellowish-green body of adults. At approximately half a millimeter in body length, they are barely visible to the naked eye and require a ten-times hand lens for reliable identification. They feed by piercing individual leaf cells and extracting the cellular contents, creating a characteristic dusty, stippled appearance on leaves that is often the first sign of infestation. In hot, dry weather they reproduce with extreme speed, with one female capable of producing over one hundred eggs and a population capable of doubling every three to five days. Their arachnid nature is critical to management because standard insecticides have little or no effect on them and often make infestations dramatically worse by killing the predatory insects and mites that naturally keep spider mite populations in check.

Spider mites are rated as high danger in hot, dry conditions. Severe infestations can kill tomato plants within two to three weeks during summer heat. They cause significant yield loss through defoliation and direct feeding damage on fruit. The speed of population increase is extreme in hot, dry weather, making early detection and rapid response essential. Resistance to multiple miticide classes is widespread in commercial production areas, complicating chemical control.

The life cycle from egg to adult takes seven to fourteen days at twenty-seven degrees Celsius and as little as five days at thirty-eight degrees. A single female produces over one hundred eggs in her lifetime. The population doubles every three to five days under hot, dry conditions, meaning that a small, early infestation can become a catastrophic outbreak within two weeks if not addressed. Within a field, mites spread by ballooning on silk threads carried by wind and can move through an entire field in one to two weeks. Cool, humid weather drastically slows reproduction and allows natural enemies to recover.

Spider mites overwinter as adult females, which turn reddish-orange in color, in soil, plant debris, and weeds around the field. They emerge when temperatures exceed twelve to fifteen degrees Celsius in spring and begin feeding and reproducing on available plant material. Wind carries tiny mites on silk threads from plant to plant and from field to field, which is the main mechanism of long-distance spread. Workers and equipment carry mites on clothing and tools within and between operations. Infected transplants can introduce mites directly into a clean field at planting. Weeds in and around field margins serve as persistent reservoirs for spider mite populations throughout the season. The most common cause of severe outbreaks in commercial production is the use of broad-spectrum insecticides such as pyrethroids, which kill the predatory insects and mites that naturally regulate spider mite populations while having little effect on the mites themselves.

Tetranychus urticae is a member of the class Arachnida, possessing eight legs as an adult, compared to the six legs of insects. This biological distinction explains why insecticides, which are designed to affect insect-specific biological processes, have limited efficacy against spider mites. The two dark spots on adults are accumulated gut contents rather than pigmentation. Adults live approximately three to four weeks and females lay one hundred to two hundred eggs during their lifetime. Eggs are laid on the underside of leaves and are spherical, pale, and shiny. Larvae hatch with six legs and transition through two nymphal stages before reaching the eight-legged adult form. The entire life cycle can be completed in five days at thirty-eight degrees Celsius, which explains why populations explode so dramatically during heat waves. Over twenty generations can occur in a single growing season. The species has an extremely broad host range, infecting over eleven hundred plant species across more than one hundred and forty plant families.

Early infestations produce tiny yellow or white stippling dots on the upper leaf surface, each dot representing a single cell drained by mite feeding. This stippling may initially be confused with thrips damage or nutrient deficiency, and confirmation requires checking the leaf underside for the mites themselves.

As populations grow, the stippling coalesces and leaves develop a bronze, silver, or dusty overall appearance from the accumulated cell damage across large areas of the leaf surface.

Heavy infestations produce fine silk webbing on leaf undersides and between leaves. This webbing confirms a large, established colony and indicates that populations are already at damaging levels. Leaves under heavy webbing turn entirely yellow and then brown and die, and the plant defoliates progressively from the bottom up.

In the most severe cases during summer heat, plants can collapse and die within two to three weeks of the infestation becoming established.

Hot temperatures between twenty-seven and thirty-eight degrees Celsius dramatically accelerate reproduction. Low humidity below fifty percent suppresses natural enemies and allows mite populations to grow unchecked. Drought-stressed plants are significantly more susceptible than well-watered plants because plant defenses are compromised and stressed tissue provides more concentrated nutrients. The disease is primarily a summer problem in field production and a year-round problem in greenhouses. The use of broad-spectrum insecticides is itself a major favorable condition because it eliminates natural biological controls.

Overwintering adult females emerge from soil and debris in spring when temperatures exceed twelve to fifteen degrees Celsius. They begin laying eggs on leaf undersides immediately. Eggs hatch within three to five days at warm temperatures. Larvae feed and develop through two nymphal stages to reach adulthood within seven to fourteen days at twenty-seven degrees Celsius. Adults mate and females begin laying new eggs within hours of reaching maturity. As populations become dense, mites produce silk and disperse by ballooning on air currents to new plants. In late season and autumn, mated females develop the reddish-orange winter coloration and move to overwintering sites in soil, bark crevices, and plant debris.

Maintaining consistent, adequate irrigation is one of the most important cultural practices for spider mite management because drought-stressed plants are far more susceptible and recover more slowly from feeding damage. Well-watered plants show greater tolerance and support faster recovery after mite populations are brought under control.

Preserving natural enemies is the single most important practice for long-term spider mite management. Avoiding broad-spectrum insecticides including pyrethroids, organophosphates, and carbamates preserves the populations of predatory mites and insects that naturally regulate spider mite numbers. Eliminating these natural controls is the primary cause of serious mite outbreaks in commercial production.

Controlling weeds around field margins removes the reservoir populations that supply mites to the crop throughout the season. Removing the most heavily infested plant material reduces the population load. Reflective silver mulches on the soil surface disorient and deter mite colonization of plants.

Biological control using predatory mites is the preferred approach in integrated pest management programs. Phytoseiulus persimilis is a highly effective specialist predator of spider mites that requires spider mite prey to survive. Neoseiulus californicus is more heat-tolerant and can persist at lower prey densities, making it better for preventive releases. Amblyseius andersoni is very heat-tolerant and performs well in summer conditions. Combining Phytoseiulus persimilis for fast knockdown with Neoseiulus californicus for persistence provides the best results. Releases should be made at the first sign of infestation and no miticides should be applied after release for a minimum of four to six weeks.

Chemical control requires miticides or acaricides specifically labeled for spider mites. Standard insecticides must not be used. Abamectin is highly effective and works as a neurotoxin but has limited ovicidal activity. Spiromesifen has excellent activity against eggs and larvae but no adult knockdown and must be applied when eggs and juveniles are present. Etoxazole and hexythiazox are mite growth inhibitors effective against eggs and larvae but not adults and must be used early in the infestation. Fenpyroximate provides fast adult knockdown but no ovicidal activity. Bifenazate has good adult knockdown. These chemistries must be rotated with every application using the IRAC resistance group numbers as a guide because resistance develops extremely rapidly in spider mite populations. Two sequential applications five to seven days apart are needed to catch eggs hatching after the first treatment. Thorough coverage of leaf undersides is mandatory because that is where mites live and feed and top-only spraying is ineffective. Horticultural oil and insecticidal soap are contact killers that suffocate mites and eggs on direct contact, require excellent coverage, and are suitable for organic programs. They have no residual activity.

When first stippling appears on a few leaves, apply predatory mites if available and apply neem oil or insecticidal soap as a bridge treatment. When bronze or silver leaves with webbing are present, apply a miticide immediately with thorough underside coverage and check the entire field for spread. When hot, dry weather above thirty degrees Celsius coincides with any stippling, act immediately because populations will double every three to five days in that weather. After any broad-spectrum insecticide application, expect a mite outbreak and monitor daily. When plants are heavily infested, remove and destroy the worst plants and treat surrounding plants with both miticide and predatory mite releases.

Scout weekly from transplanting and increase to twice weekly during hot, dry weather above twenty-seven degrees Celsius. Sample a minimum of thirty leaves per field from multiple locations throughout the field. Focus on older mid-canopy leaves and field margins where infestations typically start. Use a ten-times hand lens to count motile mites per leaf. An economic threshold of five to ten motile mites per leaf is commonly used for field crops as a trigger for treatment. In greenhouses, treat when mites are first detected on ten percent of plants. Check for the presence of predatory mites before deciding on chemical treatment. If predatory mites are active and populations are not increasing, it may be possible to hold off on chemical applications and allow biological control to take effect.

Spider mites are present globally on every continent where crops are grown. They are a major pest in Mediterranean Europe, the Middle East, North Africa, the United States, Latin America, and Southeast Asia. They are a year-round problem in tropical and subtropical regions and a seasonal summer problem in temperate climates. In greenhouses, populations are present year-round regardless of outdoor climate.

I sprayed insecticide but the mite problem got worse. Why? Insecticides kill the predatory insects that control mites. Spider mites are arachnids, not insects, and most insecticides have little effect on them while eliminating their natural enemies. Use miticides, not insecticides.

How do I check if I have spider mites? Use a ten-times hand lens and examine the leaf underside for tiny moving dots, eggs, and feeding marks. Alternatively, hold a leaf over white paper and tap sharply. Mites fall onto the paper and can be seen moving as tiny specks.

The webbing is everywhere. Is it too late? Heavy webbing means a serious established infestation. Apply a miticide with thorough underside coverage immediately and remove and destroy the most heavily infested plants to reduce the overall population load.

Can spider mites spread to other crops in my farm? Yes. They spread by wind, on clothing, on tools, and by walking between nearby plants. Check all crops in and around the affected area immediately.

Do spider mites survive winter? Adult females overwinter in soil, plant debris, and weeds. Remove crop debris and control weeds to reduce overwintering populations.

Will rain or overhead irrigation help get rid of mites? Yes. Rain physically knocks mites off leaves and raises humidity, which slows reproduction and favors natural enemies. This is why mites are primarily a dry-season problem. Overhead misting in greenhouses can help but must be balanced against disease risk from wet foliage.

Target Spot is a fungal disease of tomato caused by Corynespora cassiicola, a pathogen with one of the broadest host ranges of any plant pathogen, infecting over four hundred plant species across more than one hundred families. On tomato it produces circular to irregular brown lesions with concentric rings resembling a target or bullseye on leaves, stems, and fruit. Unlike Early Blight, which almost exclusively attacks older lower leaves, Target Spot infects leaves of all ages simultaneously, which is one of the key features distinguishing it in the field. It also infects fruit directly, a characteristic Early Blight lacks on tomato, making Target Spot capable of more direct economic damage to the marketable product. The disease has been increasing in importance globally over the past two decades, particularly in greenhouse production and tropical regions, partly due to the wide host range that provides persistent inoculum from many crop and non-crop sources in the environment.

Target Spot is rated as moderate to high danger. It does not kill plants but severe defoliation combined with direct fruit infection can cause yield losses of twenty to fifty percent in severe cases. The damage from fruit lesions is particularly significant economically because it directly affects the marketable product. The disease is increasing in importance and was previously considered minor but is now recognized as a serious threat in many production systems worldwide.

The incubation period from infection to visible symptoms is five to seven days. The disease can affect plants at any growth stage and accumulates progressively over weeks. Fruit infection is most damaging when it occurs on green, expanding berries. The speed of spread is moderate but broader than many tomato diseases because the wide host range means inoculum is consistently available from multiple sources.

Target Spot overwinters in infected plant debris in the soil as conidia and mycelium. Conidia are released during wet weather and dispersed by wind and rain splash to leaves, stems, and fruit. The fungus penetrates through stomata or directly through the cuticle and can infect tissue of any age, unlike Septoria and Early Blight which preferentially infect older tissue. Between plants, wind-dispersed conidia carry the disease throughout the crop. The extremely wide host range of Corynespora cassiicola means that inoculum can originate from neighboring crops, ornamental plants, and weeds in addition to infected tomato debris. This makes inoculum management more complex than for tomato-specific pathogens because eliminating the disease from tomato debris does not eliminate the environmental inoculum pool. In greenhouses, workers and tools spread conidia and high humidity enables rapid spread between plants.

Corynespora cassiicola is an ascomycete fungus producing very distinctive conidia that are extremely large, up to five hundred micrometers long, multicellular, and cylindrical with a long tapering beak. Under a microscope the conidia are unmistakable and confirm identification definitively. The sexual stage is rarely observed in practice and the disease propagates almost entirely through asexual conidia. The pathogen survives in infected plant debris in soil, though its persistence is shorter than some other debris-inhabiting pathogens. Pathogenic races have been described based on virulence differences, though the race structure is less well characterized than in many other pathogens. Resistance to benzimidazole fungicides such as carbendazim has been documented in populations worldwide, and strobilurin resistance has also been reported, making fungicide rotation critical.

On leaves, Target Spot produces circular to slightly irregular lesions with distinct concentric rings giving the target or bullseye appearance. Lesion size typically ranges from one to two centimeters and can be larger, which is substantially bigger than Septoria lesions. The color is brown to dark brown with a yellow halo. Unlike Early Blight which mainly affects older lower leaves, Target Spot infects leaves at all ages and positions in the canopy simultaneously. In advanced cases a gray center develops in lesions as the fungus sporulates on the dead tissue. Severe infection causes complete defoliation beginning in the lower canopy and progressing upward.

On fruit, Target Spot produces dark, sunken, circular lesions with the same concentric ring pattern seen on leaves. These lesions can penetrate into the flesh and create entry points for secondary bacterial and fungal rots. Significant direct fruit loss is possible, which distinguishes Target Spot from Early Blight and Septoria that rarely infect tomato fruit.

On stems and petioles, dark elongated lesions form and can girdle tissue causing dieback. Stem cankers are more commonly observed in greenhouse production where plants are larger and stem tissue is more exposed.

Target Spot thrives in temperatures between twenty-five and thirty-two degrees Celsius with an optimum around twenty-eight degrees. High humidity and extended leaf wetness periods are required for infection. The disease is most severe during warm, wet periods and in greenhouse and high tunnel production where high humidity is common. Dense canopies and overhead irrigation create ideal conditions for rapid spread.

The cycle begins with conidia and mycelium surviving in infected plant debris in soil. Conidia are released during wet weather and dispersed by wind and rain splash. The fungus infects tissue of any age through stomata or direct cuticle penetration during humid periods. After five to seven days, new conidia are produced on lesion surfaces and wind and rain carry them throughout the crop. Fruit infection occurs throughout the season when conditions are favorable. At season end, infected debris returns to the soil to initiate the next season's epidemic. Because of the wide host range, conidia from non-tomato hosts in the environment contribute continuously to the inoculum pool.

Crop rotation reduces debris-based inoculum but is less effective than for tomato-specific pathogens because the broad host range means inoculum can originate from many non-tomato sources. Rotating away from cucumbers, soybeans, and other highly susceptible crops is also worthwhile. Removing and destroying infected plant debris after harvest breaks the debris-based inoculum cycle. Improving air circulation through canopy management, pruning, and adequate plant spacing reduces the humidity microclimate favorable to infection. Drip irrigation should replace overhead systems to keep foliage dry. Infected lower leaves should be removed as disease progresses to reduce canopy inoculum. Surrounding crops and weeds should be scouted and managed to reduce alternate host sources of inoculum near the field. Greenhouse structures should be sanitized thoroughly between crops.

Truly resistant commercial tomato varieties are currently limited and active research is ongoing. Check regional variety trial data for tolerance ratings as new varieties become available.

Fungicide applications should be applied preventively when conditions favor infection or at the appearance of first symptoms. SDHI products including boscalid, fluopyram, and penthiopyrad provide excellent activity and are among the most effective options available. Strobilurins such as azoxystrobin and pyraclostrobin provide good protectant activity but resistance has been reported. Triazoles such as difenoconazole and tebuconazole are systemic with curative activity. Multi-site protectants including chlorothalonil and mancozeb are important components of any program for resistance management. Benzimidazoles such as carbendazim should be avoided or minimized because resistance is documented globally. Application intervals of seven to ten days are typical, shortening to five to seven days during wet weather. Good coverage of the entire plant including fruit clusters and stem tissue is important. Modes of action must be rotated with every application to prevent resistance. A practical rotation would cycle through chlorothalonil, then SDHI plus triazole premix, then strobilurin plus mancozeb, then back to chlorothalonil.

In organic programs, copper-based fungicides provide moderate protectant activity. Bacillus subtilis products have some suppressive activity. Cultural controls including rotation, canopy management, drip irrigation, and debris removal are the foundation of organic Target Spot management.

When bullseye spots appear on leaves of all ages rather than only old lower leaves, apply a systemic fungicide immediately and remove severely infected leaves to confirm Target Spot rather than Early Blight. When fruit shows circular sunken lesions, spray immediately with an SDHI or strobilurin product and remove infected fruit. When disease appears in a greenhouse with high humidity, improve ventilation urgently, apply fungicide, and remove infected tissue. When disease is appearing simultaneously in multiple crop types nearby, scout for alternate host sources and consider expanding the spray program coverage.

Begin scouting soon after transplanting because Target Spot can appear at any plant stage. Check leaves of all ages at every scouting visit, because unlike Early Blight or Septoria, young leaves are also susceptible and this is the key field diagnostic feature. Inspect fruit clusters carefully because fruit lesions may appear before canopy defoliation becomes severe. Record the percentage of plants affected, which leaf ages are affected, and the percentage of fruit showing lesions. In greenhouses, pay attention to stem lesions at plant base and mid-stem. High-risk weather periods are temperatures between twenty-five and thirty-two degrees Celsius combined with overhead irrigation, rain, or dense fog.

Target Spot was originally considered a tropical and subtropical disease but has expanded in importance globally, particularly in protected production environments. It is now significant in Southeast Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, the Caribbean and Latin America, the southern United States including Florida and Gulf Coast states, Mediterranean greenhouse production, and northern European greenhouse cucumber and tomato production.

How do I tell Target Spot from Early Blight? Target Spot affects leaves of all ages while Early Blight mainly affects old lower leaves. Target Spot also frequently infects fruit with sunken circular lesions while Early Blight rarely infects tomato fruit directly. Both have bullseye ring patterns on leaves, making the leaf age distribution the key practical distinction. Laboratory confirmation may be needed for certainty.

Does it infect other crops? Yes. Corynespora cassiicola has an extremely wide host range including cucumbers, papaya, beans, soybeans, and many ornamentals. Practice good field hygiene between crops and scout for alternate inoculum sources nearby.

Is this disease getting worse over time? Yes. Target Spot has gained importance globally over the past two decades, likely due to a combination of warmer temperatures, expansion of protected production, and possibly increased fungicide resistance in pathogen populations reducing the effectiveness of chemical programs that previously kept it in check.

My fungicide does not seem to be working against Target Spot. What should I do? Resistance to carbendazim and strobilurins is documented in Corynespora cassiicola. Switch to an SDHI-containing product such as boscalid or fluopyram combined with a multi-site protectant such as chlorothalonil or mancozeb. Consult a local plant pathologist if control failures continue.

Does it spread in dry conditions? Spread is much slower in dry conditions because the fungus requires high humidity and leaf wetness for infection. Hot, dry wind can carry conidia over long distances but infection only occurs when humidity rises and leaf surfaces become wet.
